questions.q: A chain complex $(C_\bullet, \partial)$ is a sequence of abelian groups with maps $\partial_n : C_n \to C_{n-1}$ satisfying:
questions.explain: The defining condition of a chain complex is $\partial^2=0$, i.e. $\partial_{n-1}\circ\partial_n=0$. This guarantees $\operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}\subseteq \ker\partial_n$, so $H_n=\ker\partial_n/\operatorname{im}\partial_{n+1}$ is well-defined.

questions.q: Let $C_\bullet$ be the simplicial chain complex of the standard triangle (boundary of a $2$-simplex) with $C_0=\mathbb{Q}^3,\ C_1=\mathbb{Q}^3$. What is $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_1$?
questions.explain: The triangle is homotopy equivalent to $S^1$, so $H_0=\mathbb{Q}$ and $H_1=\mathbb{Q}$. Its one independent $1$-cycle is the loop around the triangle.

questions.q: For the torus $T^2$ with rational Betti numbers $(b_0,b_1,b_2)=(1,2,1)$, compute the Euler characteristic $\chi(T^2)=b_0-b_1+b_2$.
questions.explain: $\chi(T^2)=1-2+1=0$. Every closed orientable surface of genus $g$ has $\chi=2-2g$; here $g=1$.

hard.q: Which of the following is a valid chain complex over $\mathbb{Z}$?
hard.explain:
We need $\partial_{n-1}\circ\partial_n=0$. For option B: $0\circ 2=0$. For A: $3\circ 2=6
e 0$. For C: $\mathrm{id}\circ\mathrm{id}=\mathrm{id}
e 0$. For D: $2\circ 3=6
e 0$.

hard.q: Let $C_\bullet$ be the chain complex $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 3}\mathbb{Z}\to 0$ concentrated in degrees $1$ and $0$. Compute $|H_0(C_\bullet)|$ (the order of $H_0$).
hard.explain: $H_0=\ker(C_{-1}\to C_{-2})/\operatorname{im}(\partial_1)=\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}$, which has order $3$.

hard.q: A chain map $f_\bullet:C_\bullet\to D_\bullet$ consists of maps $f_n:C_n\to D_n$ satisfying which condition?
hard.explain: A chain map must commute with the boundary maps: $\partial^D\circ f=f\circ\partial^C$. This ensures $f$ descends to a well-defined map on homology groups.

questions.q: Select every condition that is equivalent to $0\to A\xrightarrow{f}B\xrightarrow{g}C\to 0$ being a short exact sequence.
choices: $f$ is injective, $g$ is surjective, and $\operatorname{im}f=\ker g$.
$g\circ f=0$, $f$ is injective, and $g$ is surjective.
$f$ identifies $A$ with $\ker g$, and $g$ induces an isomorphism $B/f(A)\xrightarrow{\sim}C$.
questions.hint: Short exactness needs $f$ injective, $g$ surjective, AND $\operatorname{im}f=\ker g$. Just $g\circ f=0$ gives only the inclusion $\operatorname{im}f\subseteq\ker g$.
questions.explain: Short exactness asserts $f$ injective, $g$ surjective, and $\operatorname{im}f=\ker g$ — equivalently, $f$ identifies $A$ with $\ker g$ and $g$ induces $B/f(A)\cong C$. (1) is false: $g\circ f=0$ gives only $\operatorname{im}f\subseteq\ker g$, missing the reverse inclusion. (3) is too weak. (4) describes a (very) special split case. So (0) and (2) are correct.

questions.q: For $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot n}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/n\to 0$, which summary is correct?
choices: short exact and split for every $n\ge 1$
questions.explain: $\operatorname{im}(\cdot n)=n\mathbb{Z}=\ker(\text{mod }n)$, so it is short exact. A splitting would require a homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}/n\to\mathbb{Z}$, and there is none for $n\ge 2$ (any image is torsion-free). So it splits iff $n=1$, where the sequence is trivial.

questions.q: For a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ of finite abelian groups with $|A|=6$ and $|C|=10$, compute $|B|$.
questions.explain: Orders multiply across a short exact sequence: $|B|=|A|\cdot|C|=6\cdot 10=60$. This is the group-theoretic analogue of $\dim B = \dim A + \dim C$ for vector spaces.

hard.q: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$ need not split?
hard.explain: $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$ is short exact but does not split: a splitting would require a homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}/2\to\mathbb{Z}$, which is impossible since $\mathbb{Z}$ is torsion-free.

hard.q: In the long exact sequence arising from $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, suppose $H_1(B)=0$, $H_1(C)=\mathbb{Z}^2$, $H_0(A)=\mathbb{Z}^3$, and the connecting map $\partial:H_1(C)\to H_0(A)$ is injective. What is $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}\operatorname{coker}(\partial)$?
hard.explain: $\partial:\mathbb{Z}^2\hookrightarrow\mathbb{Z}^3$ is injective (since $H_1(B)=0$ forces exactness). Its cokernel has rank $3-2=1$ over $\mathbb{Q}$.

hard.q: The five-lemma: in a commutative diagram with exact rows, if the four outer vertical maps are isomorphisms, the middle map is:
choices: an isomorphism, by a diagram chase
hard.hint: Two diagram chases — injectivity then surjectivity — both ride on the outer isomorphisms.
hard.explain: The five-lemma: with exact rows and the four outer verticals being isomorphisms, a standard diagram chase (injectivity first, then surjectivity) proves the middle map is also an isomorphism.

questions.q: Given the snake-lemma diagram with exact rows and vertical maps $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$, the connecting map $\delta$ has which source and target?
questions.explain: The snake connects $\ker\gamma$ (on the right of the top row) to $\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ (on the left of the bottom row) via the recipe lift $\to$ $\beta$ $\to$ pull back along $i'$.

questions.q: Arrange the six middle terms of the snake-lemma exact sequence in their canonical order. The sequence has the shape $0\to\bullet\to\bullet\to\bullet\xrightarrow{\delta}\bullet\to\bullet\to\bullet\to 0$.
questions.hint: Kernels first (left-to-right: $\ker\alpha\to\ker\beta\to\ker\gamma$), then the connecting $\delta$, then cokernels (left-to-right: $\operatorname{coker}\alpha\to\operatorname{coker}\beta\to\operatorname{coker}\gamma$).
questions.explain: The snake-lemma sequence is $0\to\ker\alpha\to\ker\beta\to\ker\gamma\xrightarrow{\delta}\operatorname{coker}\alpha\to\operatorname{coker}\beta\to\operatorname{coker}\gamma\to 0$. Kernels appear first (in the same left-to-right order as the original columns), the connecting $\delta$ jumps from $\ker\gamma$ to $\operatorname{coker}\alpha$, then the cokernels finish in the same left-to-right order.

questions.q: In the widget's example ($\alpha=0, \beta=\cdot 2, \gamma=0$ on $\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/4,\mathbb{Z}/2$), what is $\delta(1)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha=\mathbb{Z}/2$?
questions.explain: Lift $c=1$ to $b=1\in\mathbb{Z}/4$; $\beta(b)=2$; pull back along $i':a'\mapsto 2a'$ to get $a'=1\in\mathbb{Z}/2$. Since $\operatorname{im}\alpha=0$, $\delta(1)=1$.

hard.q: In the snake-lemma diagram, which is the correct order of steps to construct $\delta(c)\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ from $c\in\ker\gamma$?
choices: Apply $\gamma$ to $c$, lift to $B'$, then apply $\beta^{-1}$
Lift $c$ to $b\in B$ via $j$ (exactness), apply $\beta(b)$, note $j'(\beta(b))=\gamma(c)=0$, pull $\beta(b)$ back to $A'$ via $i'$, project to $\operatorname{coker}\alpha$
Push $c$ forward via $\gamma$, factor through $B$, then take $\ker\alpha$
hard.explain: The snake recipe: (1) lift $c$ to some $b\in B$ using surjectivity from $j$, (2) compute $\beta(b)\in B'$, (3) since $j'(\beta(b))=\gamma(j(b))=\gamma(c)=0$, exactness gives $\beta(b)=i'(a')$ for a unique $a'\in A'$, (4) $\delta(c)=[a']\in\operatorname{coker}\alpha$. Independence of lift follows from $\partial^2=0$.

hard.q: Which condition on $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ forces $\delta=0$ identically?
hard.explain: If $\alpha$ is surjective then $\operatorname{coker}\alpha=0$, so $\delta$ must be the zero map. Alternatively, if $\gamma$ is injective then $\ker\gamma=0$ and $\delta$ has trivial domain.

hard.q: In a snake-lemma setup with $\alpha=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\beta=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$, $\gamma=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{Z}/4}$ and exact rows being the identity sequence, what is $|\ker\delta|$?
hard.explain: With all vertical maps being identities, $\ker\gamma=0$ (since $\gamma=\mathrm{id}$, every element is in the image, kernel is trivial — but actually $\ker(\mathrm{id})=0$). Wait: $\ker\delta$ is the kernel of $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$. Since $\ker\gamma=0$ (identity is injective), the domain is $0$ and $\ker\delta=0$. But if rows are $0\to\mathbb{Z}/4\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id}}\mathbb{Z}/4\to 0$, then $\ker\gamma=\mathbb{Z}/4$, $\operatorname{coker}\alpha=0$, and $\delta=0$ with $\ker\delta=\mathbb{Z}/4$, so $|\ker\delta|=4$.

hard.q: A student is building the connecting map $\delta:\ker\gamma\to\operatorname{coker}\alpha$ in the snake lemma. Exactly one step contains a logical flaw — find it.
steps: Use surjectivity of $j\colon B\twoheadrightarrow C$ to lift $c$ to some $b\in B$ with $j(b)=c$.
Compute $\beta(b)\in B'$; by commutativity of the diagram, $j'(\beta(b))=\gamma(j(b))=\gamma(c)=0$.
By exactness at $B'$, $\beta(b)\in\ker(j')=\operatorname{im}(i')$, so $\beta(b)=i'(a')$ for a unique $a'\in A'$; define $\delta(c) = a'$ as an element of $A'$ (not of $\operatorname{coker}\alpha$).

Independence of the lift $b$: a different lift $\tilde b$ satisfies $b-\tilde b\in\ker(j)=\operatorname{im}(i)$, so $b-\tilde b=i(a)$ and $\beta(b-\tilde b)=i'(\alpha(a))$, i.e. the two choices of $a'$ differ by $\alpha(a)\in\operatorname{im}\alpha$.
hard.hint: Compare target spaces: $\delta$ must land in a quotient, not in $A'$ itself.
hard.explain: Step 4 has the bug. Defining $\delta(c)=a'$ in $A'$ itself is not well-defined because the lift $b$ was a choice, and $a'$ depends on that choice by an element of $\operatorname{im}\alpha$ (as step 5 correctly notes). The fix is exactly what step 5 sets up: define $\delta(c)=[a']\in A'/\operatorname{im}\alpha=\operatorname{coker}\alpha$, which then is well-defined.

questions.q: The five lemma concerns a commutative diagram with exact rows $A_1\to A_2\to A_3\to A_4\to A_5$ and $B_1\to B_2\to B_3\to B_4\to B_5$, with vertical maps $f_1,\dots,f_5$. What is its conclusion?
choices: If $f_2$ and $f_4$ are isomorphisms, so is $f_3$.
If $f_1,f_2,f_4,f_5$ are isomorphisms, then $f_3$ is an isomorphism.
If $f_3$ is an isomorphism, so are the others.
If all rows are zero, all $f_i$ are zero.
questions.explain: The five lemma: outer four $f_1,f_2,f_4,f_5$ isomorphisms + exact rows $\Rightarrow$ middle $f_3$ is an isomorphism. Proof is the standard two-step diagram chase (injectivity, then surjectivity).

questions.q: In the four lemma (one half of five), which hypotheses imply $f_3$ is surjective?
questions.explain: The surjectivity half of the five lemma: $f_2$ surjective and $f_4$ injective force $f_3$ surjective. The injectivity half requires $f_2$ injective and $f_4$ surjective — dual conditions, split across the two halves.

questions.q: In a ladder with exact rows, $f_1,f_2,f_4,f_5$ are isomorphisms and $\dim A_3=\dim B_3=7$ (vector spaces). What is the rank of $f_3$?
questions.explain: By the five lemma $f_3$ is an isomorphism, so its rank equals $\dim A_3=7$.

hard.q: Injectivity half of the five lemma: assume rows exact, $f_1$ surjective, $f_2,f_4$ injective. Show $f_3$ is injective. Suppose $f_3(a_3)=0$. Pick the correct next step.
steps: Push forward: let $a_4 = d^A_3(a_3)\in A_4$. Then $f_4(a_4)=f_4(d^A_3 a_3)=d^B_3 f_3(a_3)=d^B_3(0)=0$.
choices: Conclude $a_4=0$ since $f_4$ is injective; by exactness at $A_3$ there exists $a_2\in A_2$ with $d^A_2(a_2)=a_3$; pushing down yields $f_2(a_2)$ and we chase further.
Take $a_4^{-1}$ under $f_4$ and hope it equals $a_3$.
Apply the snake lemma and read off the cokernel.
hard.hint: You have $f_4(a_4)=0$; the injectivity of $f_4$ kills $a_4$, which unlocks exactness at $A_3$.
hard.explain: Injectivity of $f_4$ gives $a_4=0$, i.e. $d^A_3(a_3)=0$, so $a_3\in\ker d^A_3=\operatorname{im}d^A_2$. Write $a_3=d^A_2(a_2)$. Continue the chase with $f_2(a_2)$ and use $f_1$ surjective to finish.

hard.q: Which configuration is a counterexample showing the five lemma fails if $f_4$ is merely surjective (not injective), while $f_1,f_2,f_5$ remain isomorphisms?
choices: Take all groups trivial; the conclusion is vacuously true.
Let $A_3=\mathbb{Z}$, $B_3=\mathbb{Z}\oplus\mathbb{Z}/2$, suitably; $f_3$ can fail to be surjective even though $f_1,f_2,f_5$ iso and $f_4$ surjective.
Take $f_3$ the zero map; this violates commutativity.
The five lemma never fails, regardless of hypotheses.
hard.hint: You need a map out of $A_4$ that kills a summand of $B_3$, letting $f_3$ miss torsion.
hard.explain: The five lemma is sharp: weakening any of the four outer hypotheses can break the conclusion. One can construct a concrete diagram where $f_4$ is surjective (not injective), the other three are isomorphisms, yet $f_3$ fails injectivity because a kernel element of $f_4$ hides in $A_4$ but is not the image of anything in $A_3$.

hard.q: The five lemma in an abelian category is proved by:
choices: element-free diagram chases using subobjects / generalized elements (Mitchell's embedding theorem says it suffices to verify in $\mathsf{Ab}$)
choosing a basis in each object
Yoneda's lemma and a double limit
applying Freyd–Mitchell and then quoting Zorn's lemma
hard.hint: Mitchell's theorem lets you run the same chase you'd do in $\mathsf{Ab}$.
hard.explain: In an abelian category one either uses element-free chases with generalized elements (morphisms out of a 'test object') or, via Freyd–Mitchell embedding, reduces to $\mathsf{Ab}$ where ordinary element chases work.

questions.q: Arrange the consecutive terms of the long exact sequence in homology induced by a short exact sequence of chain complexes $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, starting from $H_n(A)$.
questions.hint: $H_n(A)\to H_n(B)\to H_n(C)\xrightarrow{\partial}H_{n-1}(A)$. The connecting map lowers homological degree by 1.
questions.explain: The LES of homology runs $\cdots\to H_n(A)\to H_n(B)\to H_n(C)\xrightarrow{\partial}H_{n-1}(A)\to\cdots$. The connecting map $\partial$ comes from applying the snake lemma degree-by-degree, and lowers homological degree by $1$ (for cohomological complexes the analogous $\delta$ raises degree by $1$).

questions.q: Use the Mayer–Vietoris long exact sequence and induction to compute $\dim_\mathbb{Q} H_n(S^n;\mathbb{Q})$ for $n\ge 1$.
questions.explain: Covering $S^n$ by two contractible hemispheres $U,V$ with $U\cap V\simeq S^{n-1}$, the MV sequence gives $H_n(S^n)\cong H_{n-1}(S^{n-1})$ for $n\ge 2$, and $H_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ directly. Inductively $\dim H_n(S^n;\mathbb{Q})=1$.

questions.q: If $B_\bullet$ is acyclic ($H_n(B)=0$ for all $n$) in $0\to A_\bullet\to B_\bullet\to C_\bullet\to 0$, the long exact sequence collapses to:
questions.explain: With the middle terms $H_n(B)=H_{n-1}(B)=0$ the sequence $0\to H_n(C)\xrightarrow{\partial}H_{n-1}(A)\to 0$ forces $\partial$ to be an isomorphism. Acyclic complexes are 'invisible' except for the degree shift they impose.

hard.q: The Mayer–Vietoris sequence for $X=A\cup B$ has connecting map $\partial:H_n(A\cup B)\to H_{n-1}(A\cap B)$. Which description of the connecting map is correct?
choices: Take a cycle $z\in Z_n(X)$, write $z=a+b$ with $a\in C_n(A),b\in C_n(B)$, then $\partial a = -\partial b \in Z_{n-1}(A\cap B)$ defines the class
Push the cycle forward through the inclusion $A\hookrightarrow X$
Apply the boundary map $\partial_n$ of the ambient complex directly
Restrict a cocycle to $A\cap B$ and take homology
hard.explain: A cycle $z$ on $X$ can be written $z=a+b$ (with chains supported in $A$ and $B$ respectively). Then $\partial a = -\partial b$ is a cycle in $A\cap B$; its homology class is $\partial[z]$. This is the snake-lemma connecting map applied to the Mayer–Vietoris short exact sequence of chain complexes.

hard.q: Using the long exact sequence of the pair $(D^2, S^1)$ and the fact that $D^2$ is contractible, compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H_1(D^2,S^1;\mathbb{Q})$.
hard.explain: The LES gives $H_1(D^2)\to H_1(D^2,S^1)\to H_0(S^1)\to H_0(D^2)$. Since $D^2$ is contractible, $H_1(D^2)=0$ and the map $H_0(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}\to H_0(D^2)\cong\mathbb{Q}$ is an isomorphism. So $H_1(D^2,S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}$, dimension $1$. (By excision this equals $\tilde{H}_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Q}$.)

hard.q: Which of the following is the correct proof-step ordering to show naturality of the connecting map $\partial$ in the long exact sequence?
choices: Show $\partial$ is a group homomorphism, then check it commutes with induced maps on homology by a diagram chase in the snake lemma
Define $\partial$ on cochains, then dualize to chains
First construct the long exact sequence, then define $\partial$ to make it exact
Use the five-lemma to force naturality without any diagram chase
hard.explain: Naturality is proved by chasing the snake-lemma diagram for the naturality square: a map of short exact sequences of chain complexes induces a map of long exact sequences, and the connecting homomorphisms commute with the induced maps because each step of the snake construction is natural.

questions.q: Which of the following is equivalent to $P$ being projective as a module over a ring $R$?
choices: $P$ is a direct summand of a free module
questions.hint: The characterizations: $P$ projective $\iff$ every surjection $M\twoheadrightarrow P$ splits $\iff$ $\Hom(P,-)$ is exact (both left and right) $\iff$ $P$ is a direct summand of a free…
questions.explain: The characterizations: $P$ projective $\iff$ every surjection $M\twoheadrightarrow P$ splits $\iff$ $\Hom(P,-)$ is exact (both left and right) $\iff$ $P$ is a direct summand of a free module. Free is strictly stronger in general (though not over a PID).

questions.q: Given $\varphi\colon M\twoheadrightarrow N$ and a map $f\colon P\to N$ with $P$ projective, the lifting property says:
choices: there exists $\tilde f\colon P\to M$ with $\varphi\circ\tilde f=f$
there exists $\tilde f\colon N\to P$ with $\tilde f\circ f = \mathrm{id}$
questions.explain: Projectivity: for any surjection $\varphi\colon M\twoheadrightarrow N$ and any $f\colon P\to N$, there exists a lift $\tilde f\colon P\to M$ with $\varphi\tilde f=f$. This is exactly the dual of the 'extension' property defining injectives.

questions.q: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is NOT projective?
questions.explain: Over a PID, projective $\iff$ free. $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not free (it has torsion), and the surjection $\mathbb{Z}\twoheadrightarrow\mathbb{Z}/2$ does not split (no homomorphism $\mathbb{Z}/2\to\mathbb{Z}$). Hence $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not projective.

hard.q: Over $R=\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(xy)$, the module $R/(x)\cong\mathbb{Z}[y]$ is projective but not free. Which invariant distinguishes projective-not-free?
choices: rank — free modules have well-defined rank equal to generator count
a module can be locally free (projective) without being globally free if $\Spec R$ is disconnected or has nontrivial line bundles
torsion — projectives can be torsion while free cannot
projectives are always free over commutative rings
hard.explain: Over rings with nontrivial Picard group (like $\mathbb{Z}[x,y]/(xy)$, whose Spec has two components), projectives can be direct summands of free modules without themselves being free. The canonical example: rank-$1$ projectives $\leftrightarrow$ invertible modules $\leftrightarrow$ elements of $\mathrm{Pic}(R)$.

hard.q: Let $P$ be a module. Which statement is FALSE?
choices: $P$ is projective $\iff$ every SES $0\to A\to B\to P\to 0$ splits
Direct sums of projectives are projective
Direct summands of projectives are projective
Infinite products of projectives are always projective
hard.hint: Sums of projectives are fine; products are not.
hard.explain: Direct sums and summands of projectives are projective, and projectivity is equivalent to every SES ending in $P$ splitting. But infinite products of projectives need not be projective: e.g. $\prod_{\mathbb{N}}\mathbb{Z}$ is not a free (or projective) $\mathbb{Z}$-module (Baer–Specker group).

hard.q: How many isomorphism classes of projective modules of rank $1$ are there over a Dedekind domain with class number $h=3$?
hard.explain: Over a Dedekind domain, rank-$1$ projectives are classified by the ideal class group $\mathrm{Cl}(R)$. If $h=|\mathrm{Cl}(R)|=3$, there are exactly $3$ isomorphism classes (the trivial class corresponds to the free module $R$; the other two are non-free rank-$1$ projectives).

questions.q: $I$ is injective iff:
choices: every injection $A\hookrightarrow B$ and map $f\colon A\to I$ extends to $\tilde f\colon B\to I$
questions.explain: Injectivity: for every injection $A\hookrightarrow B$ and map $A\to I$, there is an extension $B\to I$. Equivalently $\Hom(-,I)$ is exact (both left and right). The 'every injection from $I$ splits' form is also an equivalent formulation.

questions.q: Baer's criterion says: to verify $I$ is injective over a ring $R$, it suffices to check the extension property for:
choices: all ideals $\mathfrak{a}\hookrightarrow R$ and all maps $\mathfrak{a}\to I$
questions.explain: Baer's criterion: $I$ is injective $\iff$ every map from an ideal $\mathfrak{a}\subseteq R$ to $I$ extends to $R\to I$. This reduces an a priori enormous check (all injections of all modules) to a single, tractable one.

questions.q: Which abelian group is NOT injective as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module?
questions.explain: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, injective $\iff$ divisible. $\mathbb{Q}$ and $\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ and Prüfer groups $\mathbb{Z}/p^\infty$ are divisible; $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not ($2\cdot\mathbb{Z}/2 = 0\ne \mathbb{Z}/2$).

hard.q: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, divisibility characterizes injectivity. Why does Baer's criterion immediately yield this?
choices: Ideals in $\mathbb{Z}$ are exactly $(n)=n\mathbb{Z}$; extending $n\mathbb{Z}\to I$ to $\mathbb{Z}\to I$ means solving $n\cdot x = f(n)$ in $I$ — exactly divisibility by $n$.
Because every ideal is principal with finite cokernel.
hard.hint: Lift the generator of $(n)$ and see what's needed.
hard.explain: Baer: extension to $\mathbb{Z}$ of a map $(n)\to I$ sending $n\mapsto a$ requires $x\in I$ with $nx = a$. That is exactly divisibility of $a\in I$ by $n$. Since every ideal of $\mathbb{Z}$ is some $(n)$, injectivity $\iff$ divisibility.

hard.q: Which statement about injective hulls is correct?
choices: Every module $M$ embeds in an injective module $E(M)$, the injective hull, minimal among injectives containing $M$; the hull is essentially unique.
Only finitely generated modules have injective hulls.
Injective hulls exist only over Noetherian rings.
The injective hull is always isomorphic to $M$.
hard.explain: Every module has an injective hull (or envelope) $M\hookrightarrow E(M)$: the minimal injective extension, unique up to (non-canonical) isomorphism. Over $\mathbb{Z}$, $E(\mathbb{Z}) = \mathbb{Q}$ and $E(\mathbb{Z}/p) = \mathbb{Z}/p^\infty$.

hard.q: Let $I=\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ over $\mathbb{Z}$. How many extensions $\mathbb{Z}\to I$ exist of a given map $f\colon(2)\to I$ with $f(2)=1/4\in\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$?
hard.explain: We need $\tilde f(1)\in\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ with $2\tilde f(1)=1/4$, i.e. $\tilde f(1)\in\{1/8, 5/8\}\pmod{1}$. Both are valid extensions; there are exactly $2$.

questions.q: $M$ is a flat $R$-module iff:
questions.explain: Flat $=$ tensoring with $M$ preserves exactness. Equivalently preserves injectivity (the right-exactness of $-\otimes M$ holds automatically; flatness is the extra condition that injective maps stay injective after tensoring).

questions.q: Which implication chain is correct for modules over a ring?
questions.explain: Free $\Rightarrow$ projective (trivial: free is projective); projective $\Rightarrow$ flat (direct summand of free, and free is obviously flat). None of the reverse implications hold over general rings.

questions.q: Over $\mathbb{Z}$, which of the following is NOT flat?
questions.explain: Over a PID, flat $\iff$ torsion-free. $\mathbb{Z}/2$ has torsion; concretely, tensoring $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 2}\mathbb{Z}$ with $\mathbb{Z}/2$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, which is not injective. So $\mathbb{Z}/2$ is not flat.

hard.q: A key source of flat modules: localization. Which is the correct statement?
choices: $S^{-1}R$ is flat over $R$ for every multiplicative set $S\subseteq R$.
$S^{-1}R$ is projective over $R$ iff $S$ contains a unit.
hard.explain: Localization is the archetypal non-projective flat module: $S^{-1}R$ is always flat over $R$ (tensor product with localization preserves exact sequences), but usually not projective (e.g. $\mathbb{Q}$ over $\mathbb{Z}$ is flat, not projective). This is why flatness is the 'right' local notion.

hard.q: Lazard's theorem characterizes flat modules as:
choices: filtered colimits of finitely generated free modules
hard.explain: Lazard's theorem: $M$ is flat $\iff$ $M$ is a filtered colimit of finitely generated free modules. This is why flatness is 'locally free' in a categorical sense, and why tensor products preserve filtered colimits commute cleanly.

hard.q: Let $M = \mathbb{Z}/6$ as a $\mathbb{Z}$-module. Tensor the SES $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 4}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/4\to 0$ with $M$. What is the order of the kernel of the induced map $\mathbb{Z}/6\to\mathbb{Z}/6$?
hard.explain: After tensoring with $\mathbb{Z}/6$, the map $\cdot 4\colon\mathbb{Z}/6\to\mathbb{Z}/6$ has kernel $\{x\in\mathbb{Z}/6 : 4x\equiv 0\pmod 6\}=\{0,3\}$, of order $2$. This measures the failure of flatness of $\mathbb{Z}/6$: $\operatorname{Tor}_1^\mathbb{Z}(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)=\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(6,4)=\mathbb{Z}/2$.

questions.q: To compute the right derived functors $R^iF(M)$ of a left exact functor $F$, the standard recipe is:
choices: take a projective resolution of $M$, apply $F$, take homology
take an injective resolution of $M$, apply $F$, take cohomology
apply $F$ directly to $M$ and read off $R^iF(M)$ from its kernel
take the dual module $M^\vee$ and apply $F$
questions.explain: Right derived functors of a left exact $F$ are computed from an injective resolution $0\to M\to I^0\to I^1\to\cdots$, applying $F$ and taking cohomology of $F(I^\bullet)$. The answer is independent of the resolution.

questions.q: Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{F}_2} \operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$.
questions.hint: From $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$, applying $\Hom(-,\mathbb{Z}/2)$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{\times 2=0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, whose cokernel is…
questions.explain: From $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$, applying $\Hom(-,\mathbb{Z}/2)$ gives $\mathbb{Z}/2\xrightarrow{\times 2=0}\mathbb{Z}/2$, whose cokernel is $\mathbb{Z}/2$. So $\operatorname{Ext}^1_\mathbb{Z}(\mathbb{Z}/2,\mathbb{Z}/2)\cong\mathbb{Z}/2$, dimension $1$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$.

questions.q: What is $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/6,\mathbb{Z}/4)$ as $\mathbb{Z}/d$? Give $d$.
questions.explain: $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$. Here $\gcd(6,4)=2$.

hard.q: To compute $\operatorname{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}}(\mathbb{Z}/p,M)$ via a projective resolution of $\mathbb{Z}/p$, the correct resolution is:
choices: $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot p}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/p\to 0$, which is a free (hence projective) resolution of length $1$
hard.explain: $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot p}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/p\to 0$ is a free resolution of $\mathbb{Z}/p$ of length $1$. Applying $\Hom(-,M)$ and taking cohomology gives $\operatorname{Ext}^0=M^p$ (fixed by $p$) and $\operatorname{Ext}^1=M/pM$; higher Ext groups vanish.

hard.q: Compute $|\operatorname{Tor}^{\mathbb{Z}}_1(\mathbb{Z}/12,\mathbb{Z}/8)|$ (the order of the group).
hard.explain: $\operatorname{Tor}^\mathbb{Z}_1(\mathbb{Z}/m,\mathbb{Z}/n)\cong\mathbb{Z}/\gcd(m,n)$. Here $\gcd(12,8)=4$, so the group is $\mathbb{Z}/4$ with order $4$.

hard.q: Which of the following functors is NOT right exact (and hence genuinely requires derived functors to fix)?
hard.explain: $\Hom_R(M,-)$ is left exact but not right exact in general: $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/2\to 0$ applied with $M=\mathbb{Z}$ gives $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}$, which fails surjectivity. Its right derived functors are the $\operatorname{Ext}^n$ groups.

questions.q: A projective resolution of a module $M$ is an exact sequence:
choices: $0\to M\to I^0\to I^1\to\cdots$ with each $I^i$ injective
$\cdots\to P_1\to P_0\to M\to 0$ with each $P_i$ projective
$\cdots\to F_1\to F_0\to M\to 0$ with each $F_i$ flat
questions.explain: Projective resolutions go from the left: $\cdots\to P_1\to P_0\twoheadrightarrow M\to 0$, exact at every spot. They are used to compute left-derived functors like $\operatorname{Tor}$ and $L_iF$ for right-exact $F$.

questions.q: $\mathbb{Z}/6$ has what length-$1$ free resolution? Enter the single invariant factor appearing in the presentation matrix (a $1\times 1$ matrix).
questions.explain: The resolution is $0\to\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\cdot 6}\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}/6\to 0$. The presentation matrix is the $1\times 1$ matrix $[6]$.

questions.q: What is the global dimension of the polynomial ring $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ over a field $k$?
questions.explain: Hilbert's syzygy theorem: the global dimension of $k[x_1,\dots,x_n]$ is exactly $n$. Equivalently, every finitely generated module has a free resolution of length $\le n$, and there exist modules requiring length exactly $n$.

hard.q: Over the ring $R=k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)$ (dual numbers), the module $k=R/(\varepsilon)$ has which minimal free resolution?
hard.explain: $R=k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)$ has global dimension $\infty$: $k$ has an infinite $2$-periodic free resolution $\cdots\xrightarrow{\varepsilon}R\xrightarrow{\varepsilon}R\to k$. Consequently $\operatorname{Ext}^i_R(k,k)=k$ for all $i\ge 0$ (not eventually zero). This is a typical non-regular local ring.

hard.q: Over a PID $R$, every finitely generated module has a free resolution of length at most what?
hard.explain: Over a PID, submodules of free modules are free (Smith normal form), so a finitely generated module $M$ has a resolution $0\to R^r\to R^s\to M\to 0$ of length $1$. Hence global dimension of a PID is $\le 1$.

hard.q: Which statement about flat resolutions is correct?
choices: Flat resolutions suffice to compute $\operatorname{Tor}$ but not $\operatorname{Ext}$.
Flat resolutions suffice to compute both $\operatorname{Tor}$ and $\operatorname{Ext}$.
Flat resolutions exist only for flat modules.
Flat resolutions are unique up to isomorphism, not just quasi-isomorphism.
hard.explain: $\operatorname{Tor}_i(M,N)$ can be computed from a flat resolution of $M$ (since $-\otimes N$ is right-exact and flat modules give the acyclicity needed). $\operatorname{Ext}$ requires projective (or injective) resolutions — flat is not enough.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT required of an abelian category?
choices: Every morphism has a kernel and cokernel.
Every monomorphism is a kernel and every epimorphism is a cokernel.
questions.explain: Abelian categories need a zero object, kernels/cokernels, biproducts, and every mono/epi to be normal (a kernel/cokernel respectively). They do NOT need every object to be projective — in fact $\mathsf{Ab}$ is abelian and most objects there aren't projective.

questions.q: The Freyd–Mitchell embedding theorem says:
choices: Every small abelian category embeds fully faithfully into a category of modules over some ring.
Every abelian category is equivalent to $\mathsf{Ab}$.
Every abelian category has enough projectives.
questions.explain: Freyd–Mitchell: any small abelian category $\mathcal{A}$ admits a full exact embedding $\mathcal{A}\hookrightarrow R\text{-Mod}$ for some ring $R$. The point: element-chases in $\mathsf{Ab}$ transfer to any abelian category.

questions.q: In the derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$, a distinguished triangle $X\to Y\to Z\to X[1]$ replaces what in ordinary abelian categories?
choices: a short exact sequence $0\to X\to Y\to Z\to 0$
questions.explain: Short exact sequences in $\mathcal{A}$ lift to distinguished triangles in $D(\mathcal{A})$; the rotation/shift structure of triangles encodes long exact sequences of cohomology objects. This is the fundamental move toward derived categories.

hard.q: Which example is an abelian category?
choices: the category of groups (not necessarily abelian)
sheaves of abelian groups on a topological space
hard.hint: Abelianness is inherited objectwise in a sheaf setting.
hard.explain: Sheaves of abelian groups form an abelian category (kernels are taken section-wise; cokernels require sheafification). The other examples fail: in non-abelian groups every subgroup isn't normal; in Top monos-that-aren't-kernels exist; fields lack zero objects / biproducts.

hard.q: Which axiom of a triangulated category fails in a general abelian category $\mathcal{A}$, forcing us to pass to $D(\mathcal{A})$?
choices: existence of a shift functor $[1]$ that is an autoequivalence
the octahedral axiom, which requires mapping cones
all of the above — $\mathcal{A}$ itself is not triangulated unless $\mathcal{A}=0$
hard.explain: An abelian category is not triangulated (except the trivial one): it lacks a natural shift and distinguished triangle structure. The chain complex category $\mathrm{Ch}(\mathcal{A})$, localized at quasi-isomorphisms, becomes the derived category $D(\mathcal{A})$ — now genuinely triangulated.

hard.q: Which statement about $\operatorname{Ext}$ in the derived category is correct?
choices: $\Hom_{D(\mathcal{A})}(X,Y[i]) = \operatorname{Ext}^i_\mathcal{A}(X,Y)$ when $X,Y\in\mathcal{A}$ are viewed as complexes concentrated in degree $0$.
hard.hint: Derived category maps read off all Ext groups via shifts.
hard.explain: The fundamental identification: $\Hom_{D(\mathcal{A})}(X,Y[i])\cong\operatorname{Ext}^i_\mathcal{A}(X,Y)$ for $X,Y\in\mathcal{A}$. This is why the derived category is 'the right place' to do homological algebra: all Ext/Tor appear as hom-sets after shifts.

questions.q: On the $E_r$ page of a (cohomological) spectral sequence, the differential $d_r$ has bidegree:
questions.explain: In the standard cohomological convention $d_r:E_r^{p,q}\to E_r^{p+r,q-r+1}$, i.e. bidegree $(r,1-r)$. The next page is defined as $E_{r+1}=H(E_r,d_r)$.

questions.q: A spectral sequence $E_2^{p,q}\Rightarrow H^{p+q}$ is said to degenerate at $E_2$ if:
choices: the sequence has only finitely many nonzero pages
questions.explain: Degeneration at $E_2$ means $d_r=0$ for all $r\ge 2$, so $E_\infty=E_2$. The abutment $H^n$ is then the associated graded of some filtration with graded pieces $E_2^{p,n-p}$ — you still have an extension problem, but the entries are pinned down.

questions.q: Consider the double complex $C^{0,0}=C^{1,0}=C^{0,1}=C^{1,1}=\mathbb{Z}$, with horizontal maps multiplication by $2$ and vertical maps multiplication by $3$. Taking horizontal homology first, what is the rank of $E_1^{0,0}$ as an abelian group?
questions.explain: The horizontal map $\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{2}\mathbb{Z}$ is injective, so its kernel is $0$. Thus $E_1^{0,0}=\ker(2)=0$, which has rank $0$ (and is in fact the zero group).

hard.q: The Serre spectral sequence for a fibration $F\to E\to B$ has $E_2$-page $E_2^{p,q}=H^p(B;H^q(F))$. If $F=S^1$ and $B=S^2$, which groups are the only potentially nonzero entries on $E_2$?
hard.explain: $H^p(S^2)$ is $\mathbb{Z}$ for $p=0,2$ and $0$ otherwise. $H^q(S^1)$ is $\mathbb{Z}$ for $q=0,1$ and $0$ otherwise. So the only nonzero corners of the $E_2$ page are $(p,q)\in\{(0,0),(2,0),(0,1),(2,1)\}$.

hard.q: In the Serre spectral sequence for $S^1\to E\to S^2$ converging to $H^*(E)$, the only potentially nontrivial differential on the $E_2$ page is $d_2:E_2^{0,1}\to E_2^{2,0}$. If $E=S^3$ (Hopf fibration), this map is:
hard.explain: For the Hopf fibration $S^1\to S^3\to S^2$, the spectral sequence must give $H^*(S^3)$: $\mathbb{Z}$ in degrees $0$ and $3$. To kill $E_\infty^{0,1}$ and $E_\infty^{2,0}$ we need $d_2:E_2^{0,1}\cong\mathbb{Z}\xrightarrow{\sim}E_2^{2,0}\cong\mathbb{Z}$ to be an isomorphism.

hard.q: For the Leray–Hirsch spectral sequence of the trivial fibration $E=F\times B$ with $F=S^2,B=S^2$, the spectral sequence degenerates at $E_2$. Compute $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}} H^2(E;\mathbb{Q})$.
hard.explain: Degeneration gives $H^n(E)\cong\bigoplus_{p+q=n}H^p(B)\otimes H^q(F)$. For $n=2$: $(p,q)=(2,0)$ contributes $H^2(S^2)\otimes H^0(S^2)=\mathbb{Q}$, and $(0,2)$ contributes $H^0(S^2)\otimes H^2(S^2)=\mathbb{Q}$. Total $\dim=2$.
